cycles.name: Cycle 1 2025
cycles.econ_prompts:
Pick one company that went from startup to billion dollar valuation. Explain the financial decisions that got it there and identify which single decision mattered most.
Choose a product or service you use that appears to be free. Explain how the company behind it makes money and argue whether the user is really getting a good deal.
Choose one financial crisis from the last 30 years. Explain what caused it, how it spread, and what one regulation introduced afterwards was designed to prevent.
cycles.eng_prompts:
Choose one engineering failure (a bridge collapse, a building fault, a product recall). Explain what went wrong technically and what should have been done differently.
Pick one material that changed an industry (carbon fibre, silicon, reinforced concrete, or another). Explain the properties that made it useful and how the industry worked before it existed.
Choose one system that converts energy from one form to another (a power station, a car engine, a solar panel). Explain where energy is lost in the process and propose one realistic improvement.

cycles.name: Cycle 2 Spring 2026
cycles.econ_prompts:
Pick one industry that has been disrupted by fintech (payments, lending, insurance, or another). Explain what the traditional model looked like, what replaced it, and who lost out in the change.
Choose one country whose currency lost significant value in the last decade. Explain what caused the decline and how it affected ordinary people's purchasing power.
Pick one investment that most people consider safe (property, gold, government bonds, or another). Argue whether it is actually as safe as people believe, using evidence.
cycles.eng_prompts:
Choose one city or country facing a serious water shortage. Explain the engineering solutions available and argue which one best balances cost, scale, and environmental impact.
Pick one technology that exists today but is too expensive for widespread use (space travel, hydrogen fuel, lab grown meat, or another). Explain what engineering breakthroughs would need to happen to bring the cost down.
Choose one structure or machine built more than 100 years ago that still functions today. Explain what made it last and what modern engineers can learn from its design.

text.home_tagline: Bridging Finance and Engineering for Students Worldwide
text.home_intro: Founded by students in 2025 and run through school chapters across the UAE. Finance students and engineering students work the same problems here, from budget caps to load limits.
